model_narrative: We assign a collection of items to a set of resources. For each item we decide which single resource will handle it, and we also track the total cost that results from all the assignments we make. Every resource has a limited amount of capacity, and handling an item consumes some of the resource's capacity and incurs a cost that both depend on which resource is chosen. The objective is to minimize the total assignment cost.

sets.name: i
sets.doc: the set of resources (agents) available to carry out work

sets.name: j
sets.doc: the set of items (tasks) that each have to be carried out

params.name: a
params.doc: the amount of a resource's capacity consumed when a given item is handled by that resource, in capacity units

params.name: f
params.doc: the cost incurred when a given item is assigned to a given resource, in cost units

params.name: b
params.doc: the total capacity available at each resource, in capacity units

vars.name: x
vars.doc: assignment indicator; equals 1 if the item is assigned to the resource and 0 otherwise

vars.name: z
vars.doc: the total cost of all assignments, in cost units

constraints.name: a[i,
constraints.intent: Each resource has a limited amount of capacity, and handling an item draws on that resource's capacity by an amount that depends on the item. For each resource, the total capacity consumed by the items assigned to it must not exceed the capacity available at that resource.

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: Every item has to be handled, and no item may be split across resources. For each item, it must be assigned to exactly one resource.

constraints.name: z
constraints.intent: The total assignment cost accounts for every assignment that is made. Set the total cost equal to the sum over all items and resources of the cost of assigning that item to that resource whenever the assignment is in effect.

query: Resource r1 is shouldering three items in the current plan and we'd like to keep any single resource from getting too crowded. There's a proposal on the table to cap r1 so it hosts at most two items. If we put that ceiling in place and let the rest of the network re-shuffle, where does the total assignment cost settle?
expected_pyomo: model.r1_max2 = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x['r1', j] for j in model.j) <= 2)

query: We're looking at a utilization mandate: leadership doesn't want any resource sitting half-empty, so every resource would have to be loaded to at least 85 percent of its own capacity. If we hold each resource to that floor across the board, what total assignment cost does the plan come back with?

query: There's a two-part proposal we want to stress-test together: ease the crowding on resource r1 so it carries no more than two items, while at the same time bringing resource r5 up to pull at least three. We'd run both changes at once — with that pair of rules layered in, what does the total assignment cost come out to?
expected_pyomo:
model.cap_r1 = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x['r1', j] for j in model.j) <= 2)
model.fill_r5 = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x['r5', j] for j in model.j) >= 3)

query: Resource r4 is carrying both item i3 and item i8 in the optimal plan, and stacking those two on the same resource feels like it's asking for trouble if r4 ever hiccups. Couldn't we just keep them apart — at most one of i3 or i8 on r4? What's the model seeing that makes pairing them up the better call?

query: Resource r2 is sitting way under-loaded while r4 is doing a lot of the heavy lifting in the chosen plan — that lopsidedness bugs me. Shouldn't r2 at least carry as much workload as r4, measured by the capacity its items actually consume? What's the tradeoff keeping the optimizer from levelling those two out?
expected_pyomo: model.bal_r2_r4 = Constraint(expr=sum(model.a['r2', j] * model.x['r2', j] for j in model.j) >= sum(model.a['r4', j] * model.x['r4', j] for j in model.j))

query: Same complaint on the other end of the floor: r5 looks lightly worked next to r3, which is jammed right up against its ceiling. Wouldn't it be fairer for r5 to shoulder at least as much consumed capacity as r3 does? What's the model dodging by leaving r5 the lighter of the two?
expected_pyomo: model.bal_r5_r3 = Constraint(expr=sum(model.a['r5', j] * model.x['r5', j] for j in model.j) >= sum(model.a['r3', j] * model.x['r3', j] for j in model.j))

query: Item i8 is sitting on the already-busy r4, while r2 and r5 are both running light and either one could clearly take it. Shouldn't i8 be moving off r4 onto one of those two lighter resources to even out the load? What's the model seeing that makes leaving it on r4 the better trade?
